rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank. Maryland must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt). Calibrated to methodology anchors and to states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, Alaska 47.5, Colorado 52.5, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0. Maryland at 55.6 sits between Pennsylvania and New York. Composite verified with computeCompositeFromDimensions in site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs. Input dimensions AWR 3.8, EMP 3.0, ACT 3.4, EQU 3.4, BND 2.6, ACC 3.2, SYS 4.0, INT 2.4; mean 3.225; base composite 55.625; stdDev 0.5142 so consistencyMult 1.0; 7 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. Function returned composite 55.6, band Functional, integrationPremium 0. Sensitivity case also run through the same function, not computed by hand: with SYS at 3.8 instead of 4.0 (making all 8 dimensions weak) the function returns composite 55, band Functional, integrationPremium 0. The result is insensitive to that boundary because the weakness factor is already zero at 7 weak dimensions.
attribution_note: Scores Maryland state conduct only. Maryland lost roughly 25,000 federal jobs during 2025, the most of any state; those federal terminations are NOT scored against Maryland. What is scored is Maryland’s own discretion in response — the federal public servants resource portal launched February 2025, the Protect Our Federal Workers Act signed April 2025, the fast-track state hiring program for former federal workers, and the zero-interest emergency loan program. Immigration items are scored because Maryland exercised genuine discretion: the 2025 Maryland Values Act restricting immigration enforcement in non-public areas of schools, libraries and health facilities, and the February 2026 emergency laws banning 287(g) agreements and requiring all existing agreements to end by July 2026. Federal immigration policy itself is not scored. The state budget deficit is partly federally driven, but Maryland’s allocation choices within that deficit — cutting the Developmental Disabilities Administration two years running while more than doubling eviction-prevention funding from $5 million to $11 million — are entirely state decisions and are fully scored. The Child Victims Act damages cap rollback and the absence of any budgeted settlement funding are state legislative choices and are fully scored.
key_evidence: In April 2025 Maryland reduced the damages cap for child sex abuse claims against government units from $890,000 to $400,000 per claimant, applying to previously time-barred claims not filed before 2025-06-01 — the state cut its own repair obligation after the harm was established. 12,305 claims have been filed against the state and no money is set aside in the budget to pay them.

Maryland cut the Developmental Disabilities Administration in two consecutive budgets: $164 million in fiscal 2026 and $126 million in fiscal 2027, the latter reduced from the $150 million the Governor proposed after sustained advocacy. Because these are Medicaid waiver dollars, advocates put the fiscal 2027 total service loss above $250 million once the federal match is lost.

The Maryland General Assembly overrode the Governor’s veto on 2025-12-16, by 93-35 in the House and 31-14 in the Senate, to create a Reparations Commission; the Governor’s May 2025 veto letter argued there had been enough study of the legacy of slavery.

Maryland is the first state to require a Tenants’ Bill of Rights in every residential lease, effective 2025-07-01, and the second state to guarantee counsel in eviction cases; the eviction counsel program was estimated to avoid $46 million in downstream homelessness, Medicaid and crime costs in 2023-2024.
Maryland opioid deaths hit a 10-year low in 2025, down 57% from the pandemic peak and about 26% below 2024, and the state launched a public dashboard tracking opioid settlement spending in June 2026.

Understaffing cost Maryland $350 million for 6.7 million overtime hours in fiscal 2025; the state’s largest union rejected an average 2% pay offer and close to 1,000 employees applied for buyouts in 2025.
